_doc: Cross-cutting taxonomy: concepts that appear in all three camps but carry different interpretations. These nodes are referenced by all three POV taxonomy files.

nodes.label: AGI Timelines
nodes.description: When will Artificial General Intelligence be achieved? All three camps reference this; interpretations diverge radically on both the timeline and its implications.

interpretations.accelerationist: AGI within 5-10 years; an urgent reason to invest now and shape its development.
interpretations.safetyist: AGI within 5-10 years; an urgent reason for alignment research before capability overhang.
interpretations.skeptic: AGI timelines are speculative hype that distracts from present, measurable harms.

nodes.label: The Alignment Problem
nodes.description: The challenge of ensuring AI systems reliably pursue intended goals. Central to all camps; urgency and solvability are contested.

interpretations.accelerationist: A real but tractable engineering problem; solving it is one more reason to move fast with capable labs leading.
interpretations.safetyist: An existential-level unsolved problem that must be resolved before further capability deployment.
interpretations.skeptic: A useful framing for future risk that is used to avoid accountability for alignment failures happening today (bias, misuse).

nodes.label: AI Governance and Regulation
nodes.description: All camps want 'good governance'; their definitions of what that means are largely incompatible.

interpretations.accelerationist: Light-touch regulation that does not stifle innovation; industry self-governance preferred.
interpretations.safetyist: Strong international coordination to pause or constrain frontier model development.
interpretations.skeptic: Immediate domestic regulation targeting deployed systems and their discriminatory effects.

nodes.label: Harm
nodes.description: Core vocabulary used by all three camps. The word 'harm' means entirely different things across POVs — different time horizons, different subjects, different severity thresholds.

interpretations.accelerationist: Harm = failing to deploy AI, thereby allowing preventable disease/poverty to continue.
interpretations.safetyist: Harm = catastrophic or irreversible outcomes from misaligned advanced AI.
interpretations.skeptic: Harm = documented, present-day discriminatory outcomes and labor displacement affecting real people now.

nodes.label: Risk
nodes.description: Same word; radically different probability estimates, time horizons, and moral weight across camps.

interpretations.accelerationist: Risk = low-probability tail events; present opportunity cost of not deploying AI is the dominant risk.
interpretations.safetyist: Risk = high-probability catastrophic outcome if alignment is unsolved before AGI; expected value dominates.
interpretations.skeptic: Risk = near-certain ongoing harm to marginalized groups from currently deployed systems.

nodes.label: AI Accuracy and Reliability as a Deployment Criterion
nodes.description: All three camps invoke the accuracy or reliability of AI systems when arguing about deployment decisions, but they disagree fundamentally on what accuracy measures, what threshold is sufficient, and whose errors count. The word "accurate" creates the illusion of a shared, technical standard while concealing incompatible underlying commitments about risk, interpretability, and distributional fairness.

interpretations.accelerationist: Aggregate task accuracy on benchmarks (e.g., >90%) is a sufficient condition for deployment; performance at scale justifies moving forward.
interpretations.safetyist: Benchmark accuracy is necessary but not sufficient; without interpretability of internal mechanisms, high accuracy on known tasks provides no guarantee of reliable behavior in novel or high-stakes contexts.
interpretations.skeptic: Aggregate accuracy figures obscure disparate error rates across subgroups; a model achieving 90% overall may systematically fail on statutes affecting immigrants, low-income workers, or other marginalized populations — and those failures cause the most harm.

nodes.label: Government-led AGI 'Project
nodes.description: The proposal that, due to national security implications, the US government will and should take control of the race to AGI, consolidating private labs into a national effort akin to the Manhattan Project.

interpretations.accelerationist: A necessary mobilization to ensure America builds AGI first and maintains its geopolitical leadership over authoritarian rivals.
interpretations.safetyist: A necessary step to implement state-level security, manage catastrophic safety risks during the intelligence explosion, and establish a sane chain of command for superintelligence.
interpretations.skeptic: A dangerous militarization and concentration of power that bypasses democratic oversight and accountability, while focusing on speculative future threats instead of present-day harms.

nodes.label: Intelligence Explosion via Automated AI Research
nodes.description: The hypothesis that AGI-level systems will automate AI research, creating a recursive feedback loop that compresses decades of algorithmic progress into a short period, leading to a rapid transition to superintelligence.

interpretations.accelerationist: The ultimate outcome of the AGI race, unlocking unprecedented scientific and economic progress.
interpretations.safetyist: A period of extreme volatility and risk, where alignment techniques could catastrophically fail and control could be lost.
interpretations.skeptic: A speculative, sci-fi narrative that distracts from present-day AI harms and is used to justify extreme measures.

nodes.label: Geopolitical Competition as Primary AI Driver
nodes.description: The view that the primary lens for AI development and governance should be the strategic competition between democratic nations and authoritarian rivals, with the outcome determining the future world order.

interpretations.accelerationist: The core justification for accelerating AI development and mobilizing national resources to ensure victory.
interpretations.safetyist: A major risk factor that creates pressure to race, but also a reason to secure a lead to create a margin for safety.
interpretations.skeptic: A dangerous militarization of technology that concentrates power and diverts attention and resources from addressing domestic harms.

nodes.label: Competitive Pressure vs. Safety Thresholds
nodes.description: The tension between maintaining strict, pre-defined safety standards and the strategic pressure to accelerate development or deployment in response to less-cautious competitors in the AI landscape.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is a necessary, if overly cautious, acknowledgment that racing dynamics are real and that rigid safetyism can lead to ceding the frontier to irresponsible actors.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a dangerous loophole that could justify lowering safety standards under competitive pressure, though the proposed safeguards attempt to mitigate this risk.
interpretations.skeptic: This highlights how the entire safety narrative is intertwined with corporate competition and market positioning, rather than being a pure commitment to public good.

nodes.label: Sharing Models vs. Keeping Secrets
nodes.description: This debate centers on whether making AI code public helps everyone stay safe or creates a dangerous tool for bad actors. Think of it like deciding whether to publish a blueprint for a new type of lock: it could help people improve security, or it could show thieves exactly how to break in.

interpretations.accelerationist: Releasing model details lets the global community test for bugs and fix them, making the technology safer for everyone.
interpretations.safetyist: AI models are like powerful weapons that should be kept under lock and key to prevent them from being used by dangerous groups.
interpretations.skeptic: We should focus less on hiding the code and more on checking how these models actually affect people's lives and fairness.

nodes.label: AI Agents vs. Human Control
nodes.description: As AI moves from just answering questions to performing tasks on its own, people disagree on whether this is a massive upgrade or a loss of human oversight. It is like the difference between a car you drive and a self-driving car that might decide where to go without your input.

interpretations.accelerationist: Giving AI the ability to act on its own is a huge step forward that makes the technology much more useful.
interpretations.safetyist: When AI starts making its own plans and taking actions, it becomes a serious risk that we might not be able to stop.
interpretations.skeptic: The real problem with autonomous AI is that it threatens our privacy and makes it impossible to opt out of systems we don't want to use.

nodes.label: AI as a Research Tool
nodes.description: Experts are split on whether using AI to analyze data helps us discover new facts or just repeats old mistakes. Imagine using a calculator that is great at math but sometimes makes up its own rules; you have to decide if the results are reliable or just biased.

interpretations.accelerationist: AI can help researchers process information faster, making expert knowledge available to everyone.
interpretations.safetyist: Using AI to do research without proper checks could lead to dangerous errors or scientific instability.
interpretations.skeptic: AI often hides its biases in the data it processes, which can trick researchers into thinking their biased results are actually objective facts.

nodes.label: Accuracy vs. Human Values
nodes.description: Getting an AI to be 'correct' on a test is not the same as getting it to act in a way that is good for society. It is like a student who gets an A on every test by cheating; they have high scores, but they are not actually learning the right lessons.

interpretations.accelerationist: If a model hits high accuracy targets, it is ready to be used in the real world.
interpretations.safetyist: High scores can be misleading because models often find 'shortcuts' to win the game without actually following the rules we want them to follow.
interpretations.skeptic: A model might look accurate overall, but it could still be failing specific groups of people, which is a major problem.

nodes.label: Old Security Rules Fail AI
nodes.description: Traditional security is about keeping people out of a building, but AI security is harder because the 'attacks' often look like normal, helpful questions. It is like trying to stop a burglar who is actually a guest with a key; the old rules for locking doors do not work anymore.

interpretations.accelerationist: We just need to build better security tools that can keep up with the new ways AI is being used.
interpretations.safetyist: AI creates brand new types of risks that our current security systems were never designed to handle.
interpretations.skeptic: Our current laws are outdated and need to be rewritten to focus on the actual harm caused by AI, not just who has access to it.

nodes.label: AI as an Economic Revolution
nodes.description: Some believe AI will change the economy as much as the Industrial Revolution did, but much faster. This is like moving from horse-drawn carriages to cars in a single year; it changes everything about how we work and live.

interpretations.accelerationist: AI will create a massive economic boom, and we should embrace this change to improve society.
interpretations.safetyist: The transition will be so fast that society might break under the pressure, leading to chaos if we do not manage it carefully.
interpretations.skeptic: This massive change will likely hurt workers and increase inequality, so we need strong laws to protect people right now.

nodes.label: Testing Models to Stay Safe
nodes.description: This is the idea that you have to build the most powerful AI possible to figure out how to make it safe. It is like needing to build a faster plane to understand how to make it crash-proof; you cannot learn the lessons without testing the limits.

interpretations.accelerationist: We must keep building bigger and better models because that is the only way to learn how to control them.
interpretations.safetyist: Only the most careful labs should be allowed to build these powerful models so they can study the risks in a controlled way.
interpretations.skeptic: This is just an excuse for companies to keep building dangerous things while pretending they are doing it for our own good.

nodes.label: The Problem of Missing Evidence
nodes.description: Policymakers are stuck between acting now on guesses or waiting for proof while the technology moves forward. It is like trying to decide if a new medicine is safe: if you wait for years of testing, people might suffer in the meantime, but if you rush, you might miss a hidden side effect.

interpretations.accelerationist: We should not wait for perfect proof; the benefits are clear, and we can fix problems as they come up.
interpretations.safetyist: Because the risks could be so huge, we must be careful and plan ahead even if we do not have all the data yet.
interpretations.skeptic: Companies use this 'lack of evidence' as an excuse to ignore the harms that are already happening today.

nodes.label: Building a Stronger Society
nodes.description: Instead of just fixing the AI, this approach focuses on making society better at handling any AI-related problems. It is like teaching people how to swim and building better levees, rather than just trying to stop the rain from falling.

interpretations.accelerationist: A strong society can handle the small bumps caused by AI, allowing us to move forward faster.
interpretations.safetyist: Being resilient is good, but it won't stop a major disaster, so we still need to focus on the AI itself.
interpretations.skeptic: We should focus on protecting people and fixing social problems rather than chasing technical fixes for future AI risks.

nodes.label: AI's Uneven Performance
nodes.description: AI can be a genius at one thing and completely fail at something a toddler could do. It is like a person who can solve complex physics equations but cannot figure out how to open a door; this 'jagged' skill set makes it hard to know when to trust the system.

interpretations.accelerationist: These uneven skills are just growing pains that will disappear as we build bigger and better models.
interpretations.safetyist: The fact that AI fails in such weird, unpredictable ways is a major safety risk that we do not fully understand.
interpretations.skeptic: These failures prove that AI is not actually 'smart'—it is just a statistical tool that we should not trust with important decisions.

nodes.label: AI Like Past Inventions
nodes.description: This view treats AI as just another tool, like the steam engine or the internet, that will change society slowly over time. It suggests that AI risks are similar to problems we already know how to fix, like unfairness or misuse, rather than a sudden, world-ending event. Think of it like learning to drive a car; we don't need to ban cars, we just need better traffic laws and seatbelts.

interpretations.accelerationist: This mindset is stuck in the past and ignores that AI will change the world much faster than any previous invention. It holds back progress by treating a rocket ship like a horse and buggy.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a dangerous mistake because it ignores that AI can think and act on its own in ways no other tool ever has. It’s like assuming a wild animal will behave just like a household pet.
interpretations.skeptic: This is the most sensible way to look at things because it focuses on real problems we can see today. It keeps the conversation grounded in facts rather than wild guesses about the future.

nodes.label: Competing to Build Safely
nodes.description: This idea suggests that if companies compete to be the safest, they will naturally build better AI to win over customers and regulators. Instead of slowing down, companies would treat safety as a way to prove their technology is the most reliable. It is like car companies competing to see who can build the safest vehicle to win the most buyers.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is a great way to keep moving fast while solving technical problems along the way. It turns safety into a feature that makes the product better.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a smart strategy to make sure everyone plays by the same rules. It forces companies to prioritize safety if they want to stay in the game.
interpretations.skeptic: This is just a marketing trick used by big companies to look good. It helps them stay ahead of smaller rivals by making it too expensive for anyone else to compete.

nodes.label: Tricking AI Systems
nodes.description: AI models can be fooled by tiny, hidden changes in data that humans wouldn't even notice. Because these systems rely on patterns, someone can intentionally feed them 'poisoned' information to make them act in ways they shouldn't. It is like putting a piece of tape on a stop sign so a self-driving car thinks it is a speed limit sign.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is just a technical hurdle that we will overcome as we build smarter, more complex models. It is a normal part of the engineering process. The universe itself manifests an optimization process governed by thermodynamics, and that AI is the next logical step in increasing the complexity and energy-harnessing capabilities of civilization. From this perspective, adversarial attacks are "fault-tolerant" challenges that will naturally be overcome through competition, decentralization, and the rapid, unrestricted development of AGI.
interpretations.safetyist: This proves that these systems are fragile and dangerous if we don't have total control over them. We need to lock them down before they cause real damage. Adversarial vulnerabilities are not merely technical bugs but are symptoms of a deeper alignment problem where the behavior of the system diverges from human intent under pressure. The Safetyist approach prioritizes the development of robust safeguards, rigorous testing protocols, and centralized oversight to mitigate risks that could range from financial fraud to catastrophic loss of life in autonomous systems.
interpretations.skeptic: This shows why we need strict rules and oversight right now. It is a clear example of how AI can be weaponized to cause immediate harm to people.

nodes.label: Missing Records for AI Data
nodes.description: AI models are trained on massive amounts of data that are often poorly labeled or organized. Because there is so much information, it becomes impossible to track where it came from or what biases it contains. It is like trying to organize a giant library where none of the books have titles or authors.

interpretations.accelerationist: The sheer amount of data is more important than perfect labels. We can fix the issues later once the models are already working well.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a huge problem because we don't know what hidden biases or dangerous ideas the AI might have learned. We are essentially feeding the AI a diet of junk food and hoping it stays healthy.
interpretations.skeptic: This lack of transparency makes it impossible to hold companies responsible for what their AI does. We need to know exactly what goes into these systems before they are used on the public.
